what: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
title: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
text: Provides estimation plotting tools exposure-response models use logistic regression binary responses.

title: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
text: can install development version erlr like :

title: Exposure-Response Tools for Logistic Regression Models
code: library(erlr) library(tibble)  lr_dat #> # A tibble: 300 × 6 #>       id  dose exposure exposure_quartile response sex    #>    <int> <dbl>    <dbl> <fct>                <dbl> <fct>  #>  1     1   100    112.  Q2                       1 Female #>  2     2     0      0   Placebo                  0 Female #>  3     3     0      0   Placebo                  1 Female #>  4     4     0      0   Placebo                  1 Male   #>  5     5     0      0   Placebo                  1 Female #>  6     6     0      0   Placebo                  0 Female #>  7     7     0      0   Placebo                  1 Female #>  8     8   100     40.7 Q1                       1 Female #>  9     9   100    204.  Q3                       1 Male   #> 10    10     0      0   Placebo                  0 Female #> # ℹ 290 more rows  mod <- lr_model(response ~ exposure, lr_dat) mod #>  #> Call:  stats::glm(formula = formula, family = stats::binomial(link = "logit"),  #>     data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #> (Intercept)     exposure   #>     0.03472      0.00740   #>  #> Degrees of Freedom: 299 Total (i.e. Null);  298 Residual #> Null Deviance:       379.1  #> Residual Deviance: 338.7     AIC: 342.7

code: mod <- lr_model(response ~ exposure, lr_dat) lr_predict(mod, lr_dat) #> # A tibble: 300 × 11 #>       id  dose exposure exposure_quartile response sex    fit_link se_link #>    <int> <dbl>    <dbl> <fct>                <dbl> <fct>     <dbl>   <dbl> #>  1     1   100    112.  Q2                       1 Female   0.860    0.146 #>  2     2     0      0   Placebo                  0 Female   0.0347   0.166 #>  3     3     0      0   Placebo                  1 Female   0.0347   0.166 #>  4     4     0      0   Placebo                  1 Male     0.0347   0.166 #>  5     5     0      0   Placebo                  1 Female   0.0347   0.166 #>  6     6     0      0   Placebo                  0 Female   0.0347   0.166 #>  7     7     0      0   Placebo                  1 Female   0.0347   0.166 #>  8     8   100     40.7 Q1                       1 Female   0.336    0.137 #>  9     9   100    204.  Q3                       1 Male     1.54     0.242 #> 10    10     0      0   Placebo                  0 Female   0.0347   0.166 #> # ℹ 290 more rows #> # ℹ 3 more variables: fit_resp <dbl>, ci_lower <dbl>, ci_upper <dbl>

what: Takes a fitted glm object as input and returns a function that will evaluate the underlying structural model with user-specified parameters or data (e.g., for VPCs or other counterfactual simulation scenarios). In principle this should work for glms more generally, not merely logistic regressions, but has not been tested except for logistic regression models Simulate from a logistic regression model — lr_simulator
title: Takes a fitted glm object as input and returns a function that will evaluate the underlying structural model with user-specified parameters or data (e.g., for VPCs or other counterfactual simulation scenarios). In principle this should work for glms more generally, not merely logistic regressions, but has not been tested except for logistic regression models Simulate from a logistic regression model — lr_simulator
text: Takes fitted glm object input returns function evaluate underlying structural model user-specified parameters data (e.g., VPCs counterfactual simulation scenarios). principle work glms generally, merely logistic regressions, tested except logistic regression models Simulate logistic regression model

title: Takes a fitted glm object as input and returns a function that will evaluate the underlying structural model with user-specified parameters or data (e.g., for VPCs or other counterfactual simulation scenarios). In principle this should work for glms more generally, not merely logistic regressions, but has not been tested except for logistic regression models Simulate from a logistic regression model — lr_simulator
text: function arguments param, data, type. param argument vector coefficients data argument data frame tibble type argument string indicating type prediction generate (defaults "response")

title: Takes a fitted glm object as input and returns a function that will evaluate the underlying structural model with user-specified parameters or data (e.g., for VPCs or other counterfactual simulation scenarios). In principle this should work for glms more generally, not merely logistic regressions, but has not been tested except for logistic regression models Simulate from a logistic regression model — lr_simulator
code: mod1 <- lr_model(response ~ exposure + sex, lr_dat) par1 <- coef(mod1) mod1_sim <- lr_simulator(mod1)  # no counterfactuals p1 <- mod1_sim(param = par1, data = lr_dat)  p2 <- unname(predict(mod1, type = "response")) # same result  # user modifies the data set lr_dat2 <- lr_dat[1:20, ] p3 <- mod1_sim(param = par1, data = lr_dat2)  p4 <- unname(predict(mod1, newdata = lr_dat2, type = "response")) # same result  # user modifies the parameters par2 <- par1 int1 <- par1["(Intercept)"] par2["(Intercept)"] <- 0 p5 <- mod1_sim(param = par2, data = lr_dat)
